articles.title: Centre notifies Women’s Reservation law; implementation only after delimitation
articles.summary: The Indian government has notified the Women's Reservation law, which reserves one-third of seats in Parliament and state legislatures for women. Implementation of the law is tied to the delimitation process, meaning it will not take effect immediately. This move aims to enhance women's political representation in governance. The notification marks a formal policy action by the Centre.

articles.title: 33% women quota law notified, rollout awaits census and delimitation
articles.summary: The government has notified a law reserving 33% of seats for women in Parliament and state legislatures. The rollout, however, depends on the completion of the census and delimitation process, with discussions underway to advance the implementation from 2034 to 2029. The move aims to enhance women's political representation in India.
articles.raw_summary: While the government cited technical reasons for notifying the law now, discussions are underway to enable its rollout by 2029 instead of the earlier 2034 timeline

articles.summary: Former Odisha Chief Minister Naveen Patnaik has raised concerns that the proposed Delimitation Bill could reduce Odisha's representation in Parliament. He argues that states like Odisha, which have successfully controlled population growth and improved human development, are being unfairly penalized. Patnaik urged Odisha's MPs to oppose the bill, highlighting its potential negative impact on the state's political voice.

articles.summary: The Punjab Assembly has passed a bill proposing life imprisonment for the sacrilege of the Guru Granth Sahib. The bill is part of a broader legislative effort, with similar proposals introduced in 2016 and 2018 but not enacted. This action reflects the state government's response to concerns over religious desecration. The bill now awaits further procedural steps to become law.

articles.title: Rajya Sabha–Chief Minister Paradox
articles.summary: Somewhere between the letter and the spirit of India's Constitution lies a curious paradox: a man can, under existing law, simultaneously hold membership of the Rajya Sabha, the upper chamber of the Union Parliament, and serve as the Chief Minister of a state. articles.title: 'Not New Practice' : Supreme Court Dismisses Plea Challenging ECI Transfer Of Bengal IAS/IPS Officers Amid Elections
articles.summary: The Supreme Court dismissed a plea challenging the Election Commission of India's (ECI) transfer of IAS and IPS officers in West Bengal during elections, stating it was not a new practice. However, the Court left open the legal question of whether the ECI requires state concurrence for such transfers. The case involves the ECI's authority and the balance of power between the central election body and state governments during election periods.

articles.title: DRP 130426: CAG Reveals Governance Failure in J&K Wetlands Management
articles.summary: A CAG report reveals that the Jammu and Kashmir government's conservation efforts for wetlands were limited to just six lakes, while 691 other lakes were ignored. The Forest Department did not identify eligible water bodies or seek assistance under central schemes. This raises concerns about governance and environmental policy implementation in the region.
articles.raw_summary: According to the report, government conservation efforts were limited to only six lakes—Dal, Wular, Hokersar, Manasbal, Surinsar, and Mansar—which were examined in detail. For the remaining 691 lakes, the Forest Department neither identified eligible water bodies nor formulated plans to seek assistance under schemes of the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF CC).

This directive is part of the case Shankar Mahto versus State of Bihar and aims to streamline the legal aid process, ensuring timely justice delivery. The ruling impacts how legal aid is administered and could influence future policy on access to justice in India.

articles.title: Composite Tax Order Not Fully Invalid if Partly Illegal: SC Allows Partial Enforcement Due to Separability of Transactions
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that a composite tax order is not entirely invalid if only part of it is illegal. The Court held that valid and invalid portions of the order can be separated and enforced accordingly. This decision impacts tax assessments and enforcement practices in India.
articles.raw_summary: The issue was whether a composite assessment becomes wholly invalid. The Court held that valid and invalid portions can be separated and enforced accordingly. articles.title: ‘Live or die, we have to vote TMC’: How SIR has left Bengali Muslims with no political choices

This has overshadowed governance complaints and left voters with limited political choices, particularly for the Trinamool Congress (TMC). The situation reflects broader concerns about minority rights and political representation in the state.

articles.title: HC Grants Interim Protection in GST FIR for Lack of Sanction & Prior Tax Ruling
articles.summary: The High Court examined whether an FIR filed under GST law without prior sanction is legally valid. It permitted the investigation to continue but restrained arrest and barred the filing of a charge sheet until further hearings. The ruling highlights the need for prior sanction under GST law before initiating such legal proceedings.

articles.title: Section 14A Disallowance Restricted to Exempt-Income Investments; ESOP Expense Allowed
articles.summary: An Income Tax Appellate Tribunal ruled that Section 14A disallowances must be limited to investments yielding exempt income and ordered recomputation under Rule 8D. The tribunal also allowed Employee Stock Option Plan (ESOP) expenses as a valid business deduction under Section 37(1), treating them as an ascertained liability rather than a notional or capital expense.

articles.title: Wife & Her Family Cannot Be Prosecuted For 'Dowry-Giving' Based On Her Complaint Against Husband For Taking Dowry : Supreme Court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that a wife and her family cannot be prosecuted under the Dowry Prohibition Act for 'giving' dowry based on their complaint against the husband for taking dowry. The bench of Justices Sanjay Kumar and K. Vinod Chandran dismissed an appeal by a husband seeking an FIR against his wife and her family. The ruling clarifies legal protections for women and their families in dowry-related disputes.
articles.raw_summary: The Supreme Court on Thursday (April 16) held that a woman or his family members could not be subjected to proceedings under the Dowry Prohibition Act for 'giving' dowry based on the averments made by them in their complaint against the 'dowry takers'. articles.title: Section 69C Addition Deleted as Purchases Supported by Documentary Evidence
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal ruled that purchases supported by documentary evidence such as invoices, bank payments, and GST records cannot be treated as bogus. The tribunal emphasized that the absence of adverse evidence makes such tax additions unsustainable. This decision clarifies the evidentiary standards for tax authorities in assessing transactions under Section 69C of the Income Tax Act.

articles.title: What lagging jet fuel supplies could mean for airlines and travelers
articles.summary: A looming jet fuel shortage in Europe and Asia, caused by the Iran war and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, threatens to disrupt global travel within weeks. This could lead to higher airfares and flight cancellations as the summer travel season approaches. The situation may have ripple effects on India's aviation industry and energy policies.
articles.raw_summary: A looming jet fuel shortage in Europe and Asia sparked by the Iran war and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz could further upend world travel within weeks if oil doesn't start flowing again soon — meaning higher airfares and flight cancellations as the summer travel season approaches.

articles.title: Why a U.S. blockade on Iran seems to be working
articles.summary: Iran-linked vessels are avoiding or turning back from the Strait of Hormuz due to U.S. sanctions, with some ships reportedly faking or jamming their locations. Shipping firms note this is disrupting an already risky maritime environment. The situation could affect global oil supplies and shipping costs, which may indirectly impact India's energy imports and trade policies.
articles.raw_summary: Iran-linked or sanctioned vessels that have left the Persian Gulf through the Strait of Hormuz have stopped or turned around, shipping data firms say. They appear to have jammed or faked their locations in some instances, complicating an uncertain and risky shipping situation.

articles.summary: The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Board of India (IBBI) has proposed amendments to improve transparency in insolvency cases involving personal guarantors. The changes aim to mandate asset disclosure by guarantors and enhance creditor oversight to ensure better resolution outcomes. The amendments address gaps in current disclosure and coordination practices in insolvency proceedings.

articles.title: Reassessment Quashed as Wrong Provision Invoked Instead of Section 153C: ITAT Jaipur
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) in Jaipur quashed a tax reassessment order because authorities incorrectly invoked Section 147 instead of Section 153C. The tribunal ruled that the reassessment was invalid as it relied on third-party search material, leading to the deletion of tax additions. This ruling clarifies the correct legal route for such cases under Indian tax law.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that reopening under Section 147 was invalid where it was based on third-party search material. It ruled that Section 153C was the correct legal route, leading to deletion of additions. The post Reassessment Quashed as Wrong Provision Invoked Instead of Section 153C: ITAT Jaipur appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: No FTS on Project-Specific Design Services; ‘Make Available’ Test Not Met, No Disallowance u/s 40(a)(i)
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal ruled that consultancy payments for architectural services do not qualify as 'Fees for Technical Services' (FTS) because no technical knowledge was 'made available.' As a result, the disallowance under Section 40(a)(i) of the Income Tax Act was deleted. The ruling clarifies tax treatment for project-specific design services in India.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that consultancy payments for architectural services were not FTS since no technical knowledge was made available. Disallowance under Section 40(a)(i) was deleted. The post No FTS on Project-Specific Design Services; ‘Make Available’ Test Not Met, No Disallowance u/s 40(a)(i) appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.summary: The Karnataka High Court ruled that businesses can claim input tax credit (ITC) beyond the invoice month due to practical accounting constraints, rejecting restrictions that could lead to cascading taxation. The ruling addresses real-world challenges in accounting practices while upholding the core objective of tax efficiency.

articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) in Dehradun deleted a penalty imposed under Section 271(1)(c) of the Income Tax Act because earlier rulings on identical facts had led to the deletion of such penalties. The tribunal applied the principle of consistency, ruling that the penalty could not be sustained. The case involved tax assessment proceedings where the assessee challenged the penalty imposed by tax authorities.
articles.raw_summary: The Tribunal held that penalty under section 271(1)(c) cannot be sustained when identical facts in earlier years led to deletion. articles.title: Appeal by Suspended Director in Corporate Debtor’s Name Not Maintainable Post-CIRP: SC
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that an appeal filed by a suspended director in the name of a corporate debtor after the Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process (CIRP) has been admitted is not legally valid. The court held that such appeals cannot be cured or converted after the limitation period under Section 61(2) of the IBC expires. The ruling overturns the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal's (NCLAT) earlier approach on the matter.
articles.raw_summary: **SEO Description:** Supreme Court holds that an appeal filed by a suspended director in the name of the corporate debtor after admission of CIRP is not maintainable. It rules that such a fundamentally incompetent appeal cannot be cured or converted after expiry of limitation under Section 61(2) of the IBC, and sets aside NCLAT’s contrary approach. The post Appeal by Suspended Director in Corporate Debtor’s Name Not Maintainable Post-CIRP: SC appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: GST Refund Risk Arises as ECGC Insurance Settlement Not Equivalent to Export Proceeds
articles.summary: The Export Credit Guarantee Corporation of India (ECGC) insurance payouts in Indian Rupees do not qualify as export proceeds under Goods and Services Tax (GST) and Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA) laws. Exporters are now at risk of losing GST refunds and facing interest liabilities unless they secure bank write-offs. This issue highlights a gap in policy interpretation that could impact India's export sector.
articles.raw_summary: ECGC payouts in INR do not qualify as export proceeds under GST and FEMA laws. Exporters must secure AD bank write-offs to avoid refund recovery and interest liability. articles.title: Faster insolvency process proposed with creditor-led resolution framework
articles.summary: The government has proposed a new Creditor-initiated Insolvency Resolution Process (CIIRP) to speed up debt resolution. Financial creditors holding 51% of the debt can now initiate proceedings directly, bypassing the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) admission process. The NCLT's role is limited to approving moratoriums and plans, with the entire process aimed to be completed within 150 days, down from the current 330 days.
articles.raw_summary: A new Creditor-initiated Insolvency Resolution Process (CIIRP) aims to expedite debt resolution, allowing financial creditors with 51% debt share to initiate proceedings. This bypasses the NCLT admission process, with the tribunal's role limited to moratorium and plan approval. The entire process is targeted for completion within 150 days, a significant reduction from the current 330 days.

articles.title: Sabarimala reference in SC: Managing board wants temple’s rights to prevail over individual’s rights
articles.summary: The Supreme Court is examining a reference related to the Sabarimala temple dispute, where the managing board's lawyer argued that the temple's rights should take precedence over individual rights. The lawyer also acknowledged the state's potential authority to open the temple to all Hindus, but only to ensure entry for all Hindu classes and sections. The case involves balancing religious customs with constitutional rights.
articles.raw_summary: However, board's lawyer did not rule out state's right to throw open public temple to all classes and sections of Hindus. Such a right would be limited only to ensuring all Hindus gain entry, he said.

articles.title: Saradha scam trials: 13 yrs on, Bengal’s biggest financial fraud stuck in a ‘stalemate’ in courts
articles.summary: Thirteen years after India's biggest financial fraud—the Saradha scam—court proceedings remain stalled in West Bengal. ThePrint's analysis of court records reveals systemic delays in the judicial process. The case, involving massive financial losses for thousands of investors, reflects broader challenges in India's legal and regulatory frameworks for financial crimes.

articles.title: Can TMC survive SIR disruptions and alliance wars in 2026
articles.summary: The article examines whether Mamata Banerjee’s Trinamool Congress (TMC) can sustain its political coalition in West Bengal amid administrative disruptions caused by the State Industrial Policy (SIR) and growing opposition alliances ahead of the 2026 elections. Political observer Sayantan Ghosh analyzes the ground reality, questioning the TMC’s ability to navigate these challenges. The analysis suggests potential implications for governance and electoral strategies in the state.

Political observer and author Sayantan Ghosh analyses the ground reality.

articles.title: US’ 301 probes: India denies allegations, seeks termination of both investigations
articles.summary: India has rejected allegations by the US Trade Representative under its Section 301 probe, denying claims of “structural excess capacity” in sectors such as petrochemicals and textiles. articles.title: India keeps fuel flowing despite Hormuz tensions; LPG ships on way, supplies stabilising: Govt
articles.summary: Despite the uncertainty, the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG) said India has restored about 70% of its commercial LPG supplies and tied up additional cargoes. The country had been heavily reliant on supplies routed through Hormuz but has, over the past 35 days, secured alternative arrangements “quite satisfactorily,” officials said.

articles.title: Iran war impact: Essential drugs may cost up to 5% more, for now
articles.summary: The price of essential drugs in India may rise by 3-5% due to the ongoing Iran war, according to industry expectations. This increase is projected to last 3-4 months, with a possible rollback if input costs stabilize. The impact is similar to recent GST rate cuts, and the industry anticipates a temporary adjustment rather than a long-term change.
articles.raw_summary: The immediate net consumer impact could be 3-5% higher prices, or roughly similar to what consumers paid before the late-September cuts in GST rates. The industry expects the price increase to be in place for 3-4 months, with a rollback option once input costs stabilise.

articles.title: Mining push meets tribal resistance
articles.summary: Tribal villagers in Odisha's Rayagada and Kalahandi districts are protesting against bauxite mining at Sijimali hill, leading to clashes with police. The administration's attempt to build a road to a mining block escalated tensions, resulting in tear gas firing and injuries. The conflict reflects broader issues of tribal displacement and environmental concerns, which may prompt policy responses from the government.
articles.raw_summary: Tribal villagers and the administration are at loggerheads over bauxite mining at the Sijimali hill, which straddles Rayagada and Kalahandi districts in south Odisha. Tension has gripped the region ever since attempts were made to lay a road to a bauxite block. On April 7, police fired tear gas shells to disperse protesting villagers and several personnel were injured in the counter-attack. Satyasundar Barik explains why it is not an isolated incident

articles.title: IAF ties up with GE to build own facility for repair, overhaul of Tejas engines with quicker turnaround
articles.summary: The Indian Air Force (IAF) will establish and operate its own depot facility for repairing and overhauling Tejas jet engines, with technical support from GE Aerospace. The facility aims to reduce turnaround time for engine maintenance. GE will provide training, spare parts, and specialized equipment, while the IAF will own and manage the facility. This move aligns with India's push for indigenous defence capabilities and reduced reliance on foreign maintenance support.
articles.raw_summary: Depot facility to be owned, operated   maintained by IAF, with GE Aerospace providing technical inputs, training, support staff   supply of necessary spares   specialised equipment.

articles.title: North Korea rapidly expanding nuclear weapons capability, UN watchdog warns
articles.summary: The UN's nuclear watchdog has warned that North Korea is making rapid progress in expanding its nuclear weapons capability, with increased activity at its main nuclear complex. The regime is believed to have assembled around 50 nuclear warheads, raising concerns about its ability to miniaturize them. This development could impact regional security dynamics, including India's strategic interests and policy considerations.

North Korea is thought to have assembled about 50 nuclear warheads, although some experts are sceptical of its claims that it is able to miniaturise

articles.title: Women's workwear: When proper fit becomes a safety issue
articles.summary: Many women in India's industrial and trade sectors face safety risks due to poorly fitting workwear, often forced to wear men's gear. This discomfort and lack of proper protection can lead to workplace hazards. Some companies are now designing safer, gender-specific workwear, but systemic gaps remain. The issue underscores broader challenges in workplace safety and gender-inclusive labour policies.
articles.raw_summary: With limited options, many women in trades and industry have to wear men's workwear. But a poor fit causes discomfort and can compromise safety. Now, some firms are stepping up to design safer, better-fitting gear.

articles.title: Trump's Strait of Hormuz blockade hits Iran's oil trade
articles.summary: The US has imposed a naval blockade in the Strait of Hormuz, halting most of Iran's oil exports. This action, led by President Trump, aims to pressure Iran but risks escalating tensions. The blockade disrupts Iran's main source of foreign currency and could impact global oil supplies, raising concerns about energy security and geopolitical stability.
articles.raw_summary: A US naval blockade cuts off Iran's main source of hard currency by halting most of its oil exports. Will President Trump's pressure force Tehran back to negotiations or widen the conflict?

articles.title: Merck's Keytruda: A lifesaving drug, a global divide
articles.summary: A cross-border investigation by DW and ICIJ reveals how pricing and patents turned Merck's Keytruda—a life-saving cancer drug—into one of the world's best-selling drugs while making it inaccessible to many patients. The report highlights a global divide in access to essential medicines, raising questions about patent policies, drug pricing, and trade barriers that may impact countries like India.

The SEZ will facilitate integrated development of electronics and IT services, aligning with national goals for self-reliance in semiconductors.

articles.title: Uber says bike taxis not eating into autos; calls for 'sensible' regulation
articles.summary: In a blog post, Uber’s policy and research team said that in Bengaluru and Mumbai — two key markets where bike taxi services were paused for nearly two months in mid-2025 — auto-rickshaw demand continued to grow at a similar pace even when bike taxis were unavailable. articles.raw_summary: Cultivated on nearly 10,000 acres, these seeds are the world’s second most important natural colourant after caramel. Thanks to impressive laboratory tests confirming seed quality and organic farming practices, they are in high demand across the European Union and the Middle East. However, the absence of government-backed organic certification, a proper procurement mechanism, and adequate storage facilities is forcing farmers to sell their produce at throwaway prices to middlemen, writes T. Appa

articles.title: IMF cuts West Asia, North Africa growth forecast to 1.1% over Iran war

The IMF warned that the conflict could disrupt regional stability and economic activity, which may have implications for global oil supplies and trade dynamics.

Higher oil prices and geopolitical instability are cited as likely consequences.

This development matters for India as it relies heavily on oil imports and faces inflation pressures from fuel costs.

articles.title: Crude oil prices will soon reflect the extent of this energy supply crisis, IEA warns
articles.summary: Brent crude oil prices dropped to $95 per barrel on Tuesday, and West Texas Intermediate (WTI) is testing $90 per barrel, down from earlier highs of $120. The decline follows hopes of resumed US-Iran talks, which could ease energy supply concerns. The International Energy Agency (IEA) warns that prices will soon reflect the severity of the global energy supply crisis. The situation may influence India's energy policies, including fuel pricing and import strategies.

articles.title: ‘Largest energy crisis’: Energy agency head warns Europe has ‘maybe 6 weeks’ of jet fuel left
articles.summary: The head of the International Energy Agency (IEA), Fatih Birol, warned that Europe may face a severe jet fuel shortage within six weeks if oil supplies remain blocked due to the ongoing conflict between the United States and Iran. He cautioned that flight cancellations could follow soon if the situation persists. The crisis highlights vulnerabilities in global energy supply chains, which could impact India's energy imports and policy decisions.
articles.raw_summary: IEA chief Fatih Birol said flight cancellations could follow “soon” if oil supplies remain blocked amid the war between United States and Iran.

articles.title: ‘These things happen’, HR official linked to TCS told woman wanting to report harassment in Nashik
articles.summary: Nashik Police have registered nine cases involving a suspected organized gang operating within a local IT branch. A woman attempting to report harassment was allegedly told by an HR official linked to Tata Consultancy Services (TCS) that 'these things happen.' The incident raises concerns about workplace safety, corporate accountability, and the handling of harassment complaints in India's IT sector.
